message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in CURRENT code (4 hardening passes applied). Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described, the PoC reproduces, and it is genuine (not speculative/already-handled/by-design). Cite file:line.

H-019: No concurrency group — superseded PR pushes keep running and audit/lockfile-compile can race
File: .github/workflows/ci.yml:1
Description: Verified: ci.yml has no concurrency block, so every push to an open PR spawns the full matrix without cancelling the prior in-flight run. Beyond wasted minutes, two overlapping audit runs on the same branch both run lockfile resolution / 'pip install -e .' and upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race. On a single dedicated compute node this compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools.
PoC: grep -n concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. Push twice in quick succession to a PR; both runs execute to completion.
Suggested fix: Add a top-level concurrency: { group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true } so a newer commit cancels the stale run and only one audit/lockfile-compile executes per ref.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual CI workflow file.

content.content:
1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
22	        run: |
23	          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
24	          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
25	          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
26	          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
27	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
28	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
29	          pip install --no-deps -e .
30	      - name: Lint (ruff)
31	        run: ruff check src tests
32	      - name: Format check (ruff)
33	        run: ruff format --check src tests
34	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
35	        run: mypy
36	      - name: Test (pytest)
37	        run: pytest -q
38	
39	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
40	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
41	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
42	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
43	  # e2e) run for real.
44	  integration:
45	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
46	    services:
47	      postgres:
48	        image: postgres:16
49	        env:
50	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
51	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
52	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
53	        options: >-
54	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
55	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
56	      qdrant:
57	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
58	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
59	    env:
60	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
61	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/assay
62	    steps:
63	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
64	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
65	        with:
66	          python-version: "3.12"
67	      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
68	        run: |
69	          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
70	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
71	          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
72	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
73	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
74	          pip install --no-deps -e .
75	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
76	        run: mypy
77	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
78	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
79	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
80	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
81	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
82	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
83	
84	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
85	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
86	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
87	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
88	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
89	  audit:
90	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
91	    steps:
92	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
93	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
94	        with:
95	          python-version: "3.12"
96	      - name: Install pip-audit
97	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
98	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
99	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
100	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
101	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
102	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
103	        run: |
104	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
105	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
106	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
107	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
108	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
109	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
110	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
111	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
112	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
113	          # stay in sync too.
114	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
115	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
116	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
117	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
118	        run: |
119	          set -euo pipefail
120	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
121	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
122	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
123	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
124	          IGNORES=()
125	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
126	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
127	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
128	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
129	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
130	          fi
131	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
132	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
133	        run: |
134	          set -euo pipefail
135	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
136	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
137	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
138	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
139	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
140	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
141	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
142	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
143	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
144	          IGNORES=()
145	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
146	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
147	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
148	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
149	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
150	          fi
151	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
152	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
153	          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
154	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
155	        if: always()
156	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
157	        with:
158	          name: sbom-cyclonedx
159	          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
160	          if-no-files-found: error
161	      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
162	        run: |
163	          set -euo pipefail
164	          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
165	          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
166	          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
167	          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
168	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
169	          pip install --no-deps -e .
170	          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
171	          pip-licenses --format=json --with-system > licenses.json
172	          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
173	      - name: Upload license report
174	        if: always()
175	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
176	        with:
177	          name: license-report
178	          path: licenses.json
179	          if-no-files-found: warn
180	
181	  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
182	  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
183	  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
184	  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
185	  all-checks:
186	    if: always()
187	    needs: [core, integration, audit]
188	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
189	    steps:
190	      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
191	        run: |
192	          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
193	          echo "job results: $results"
194	          for r in $results; do
195	            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
196	              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
197	              exit 1
198	            fi
199	          done
200	          echo "all required jobs succeeded"
201

content.text: The file has no `concurrency` block. Let me verify there are no other workflow files that might define it and confirm the artifact upload behavior.

input.description: Search for concurrency config and list workflow files

content.content:
---files---
total 24
drwxr-xr-x  3 [REDACTED]  staff     96 Jun 17 00:32 .
drwxr-xr-x  4 [REDACTED]  staff    128 Jun 17 00:32 ..
-rw-r--r--  1 [REDACTED]  staff  10797 Jun 17 00:32 ci.yml

content.text:
Confirmed. No `concurrency` block anywhere in `.github/`. Let me assess the actual severity claims.

The finding is factually accurate on the core claim. Now let me evaluate the severity-justifying claims about the artifact "race":

- Artifacts (`sbom-cyclonedx`, `license-report`) are uploaded with fixed names. However, GitHub Actions scopes artifacts per workflow run, not per ref. Two concurrent runs of the same workflow have distinct run IDs, so their artifacts do NOT collide — each run gets its own artifact namespace. So the "upload identically-named artifacts that can race" claim is technically wrong; there's no cross-run artifact corruption.

- The lockfile-compile and `pip install -e .` run in isolated GitHub-hosted runner VMs (`runs-on: ubuntu-latest`), each ephemeral and isolated. They do NOT share filesystem/state, so two overlapping runs cannot actually race on lockfile resolution or installs. The "compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools on a single dedicated compute node" claim is also wrong — CI runs on GitHub-hosted ubuntu-latest runners, not the dedicated Mac mini compute node.

So the underlying *correctness/race* concerns in the description are false. What remains true and genuine is: missing concurrency group means superseded pushes keep running, wasting CI minutes. That is a real but minor efficiency/hygiene issue — best practice, not a production-readiness defect.

input.reasoning:
The literal grep claim is true: .github/workflows/ci.yml (the only workflow file, confirmed via ls and grep across .github/) has no `concurrency` block, so a newer push to an open PR does not cancel the prior in-flight run (ci.yml:1-7 trigger on push/pull_request with no concurrency key). So superseded runs do keep executing.

However, the finding's severity-justifying claims — the parts that elevate it above trivial CI hygiene — are false:

1. "two overlapping audit runs ... upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race": GitHub Actions artifacts are scoped per workflow RUN (distinct run IDs), not per ref. Two concurrent runs have separate artifact namespaces, so the fixed names (sbom-cyclonedx ci.yml:158, license-report ci.yml:177) cannot collide or corrupt each other across runs. No race.

2. "both run lockfile resolution / pip install -e . and can race": every job uses runs-on: ubuntu-latest (ci.yml:12,45,90,188) — ephemeral, fully isolated GitHub-hosted VMs per run. Concurrent runs share no filesystem or state, so the uv pip compile (ci.yml:108-116) and pip install -e . (ci.yml:29,74,169) cannot race.

3. "compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools on a single dedicated compute node": CI executes on GitHub-hosted runners, not the dedicated Mac mini production node. There is no resource contention with local pools.

Stripped of the false race/contention claims, the only genuine residual is wasted CI minutes on superseded pushes — a best-practice efficiency improvement, not a production-readiness defect or any kind of correctness/security problem. Adding cancel-in-progress is a reasonable nice-to-have, but the finding as DESCRIBED (race conditions, node contention) does not reproduce and is not a true hardening defect. Marking not confirmed / false_positive on the substantive (non-cosmetic) claims.

content.text: Verified H-019 as a false positive. The only true part is the missing `concurrency` block (confirmed at ci.yml, the sole workflow file); all the race/contention claims that would make it a real defect are wrong because GitHub-hosted runners are isolated per-run and artifacts are run-scoped.
